questions.question: Why is a shared distributed cache (Redis) often preferred over per-instance in-process caches once you run many app servers?
options: In-process caches are slower per hit
A shared cache gives one consistent copy that all servers see and that survives app restarts, instead of N separate caches
Redis stores data on disk, which is faster than RAM
Distributed caches never need eviction
questions.explanation: Per-instance caches are separate (each cold for what others warmed, each with its own copy) and die with the process. A distributed cache provides one shared copy, one place to invalidate, and persistence across app restarts — at the cost of a network round trip.

questions.question: What is a 'cache stampede' / thundering herd?
options: When the cache server runs out of memory
When a popular key expires (or the cache is cold) and many concurrent requests all miss and hit the database simultaneously with the same query
When too many keys are stored at once
When the cache returns wrong values
questions.explanation: When a hot key expires, hundreds of concurrent requests can all check the cache, all miss, and all run the same expensive query at once — because none has finished repopulating the cache. One expired key can generate hundreds of identical DB queries and overload the database.

questions.question: How does a per-key lock (request coalescing) prevent a stampede?
options: It blocks all reads until the cache is manually refreshed
Only the first request acquires the lock and queries the DB; the others wait and then read the freshly-cached value
It deletes the key so no one can read it
It disables the cache during high load
questions.explanation: With request coalescing (single-flight), the first thread to miss acquires a lock and recomputes the value once. Concurrent missers wait on the lock and then read the value it cached — collapsing 100 would-be queries into 1.

questions.question: What does 'TTL jitter' (randomizing expiry times) help with?
options: Making the cache use less memory
Preventing many keys from expiring at the exact same instant, which would synchronize their recomputation
Encrypting the cached values
Increasing the hit ratio to 100%
questions.explanation: If many keys are set with the same TTL, they expire together and cause a synchronized wave of misses. Adding randomness (e.g. 300s ± 30s) spreads expirations over time, so recomputation is staggered rather than a thundering herd.

questions.question: Why can 'just add Redis' sometimes make database load WORSE at the dangerous moments?
options: Redis is slower than a database
All app instances now miss in lockstep on a shared expired key or a cold/failed cache, whereas independent in-process caches miss at different times
Redis corrupts data
Redis cannot store hot keys
questions.explanation: A shared cache concentrates risk: with synchronized TTLs or a cold/failed Redis, every instance misses simultaneously and floods the DB. Independent in-process caches at least miss at staggered times. The cache cuts steady-state load hugely, but the transitions (expiry, cold start, failover) need explicit handling.
